questions.subcategory: Safety and Infection Control / Stable vs. Unstable
questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient 4 hours post-cardiac catheterization via right femoral access. The nurse finds the patient diaphoretic, the right groin dressing saturated with blood, and the patient's blood pressure has dropped from 128/76 to 88/50 mmHg. What is the PRIORITY nursing action?

options.text: Apply direct, firm manual pressure to the right groin site immediately
Elevate the foot of the bed and call the provider
Obtain a STAT hemoglobin and hematocrit
Increase the IV fluid rate per standing orders and reassess in 15 minutes

questions.rationale: The patient is showing signs of femoral artery hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability (hypotension, diaphoresis, saturated dressing). The priority is hemorrhage control: applying immediate, firm manual pressure directly over the puncture site. This is the most time-critical intervention to stop ongoing blood loss. Calling the provider and obtaining labs are necessary but are performed concurrently by a second nurse after pressure is established. Trendelenburg positioning is not recommended for vascular hemorrhage, and delaying for 15 minutes risks cardiovascular collapse.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it relies on notification without the bedside safety step. "Apply direct, firm manual pressure to the right groin site immediately" fits the safer response to a nurse is caring for a patient 4 hours post-cardiac catheterization via right femoral access.
distractorRationales.c: Because after femoral cardiac catheterization, a saturated groin dressing + hypotension = vascular bleeding emergency suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, "Obtain a STAT hemoglobin and hematocrit" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.d: "Increase the IV fluid rate per standing orders and reassess in 15 minutes" misses the bedside risk because after femoral cardiac catheterization, a saturated groin dressing + hypotension = vascular bleeding emergency points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

tags: Safety and Infection Control / Stable vs. Unstable
questions.takeaway: After femoral cardiac catheterization, a saturated groin dressing + hypotension = vascular bleeding emergency. Stop the bleed first (manual pressure), then call and escalate. Hemorrhage control precedes all other actions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is planning an immunization education program. Which statement about adult immunization schedules is correct?

options.text: The Tdap booster is recommended annually for all adults
Pneumococcal vaccine (PCV15 or PCV20) is recommended for all adults 65 years and older; influenza vaccine is recommended annually for everyone 6 months and older
The shingles (zoster) vaccine is recommended for adults 40 years and older
The MMR vaccine is required annually for healthcare workers

questions.rationale: Current ACIP adult immunization priorities: (1) Influenza: annually for everyone ≥6 months; (2) Tdap: once as an adult, then Td booster every 10 years; Tdap specifically recommended during each pregnancy at 27–36 weeks gestation (protects newborn via transplacental antibodies); (3) Pneumococcal: PCV15 or PCV20 for all adults ≥65; PCV20 or PCV15 + PPSV23 for high-risk adults 19–64; (4) Zoster (Shingrix): recommended for adults ≥50 years (not 40), 2-dose series; (5) MMR: not annual; 2-dose series if no immunity; HCWs need 2-dose MMR if no evidence of immunity.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses pediatric or newborn instability; "The Tdap booster is recommended annually for all adults" moves care toward routine care before maternal-fetal stabilization.
distractorRationales.c: Because hCWs need 2-dose MMR if no evidence of immunity suggests pediatric or newborn instability, "The shingles (zoster) vaccine is recommended for adults 40 years and older" would not correct the active problem. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.
distractorRationales.d: Because hCWs need 2-dose MMR if no evidence of immunity suggests pediatric or newborn instability, "The MMR vaccine is required annually for healthcare workers" would not correct the active problem. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.

questions.takeaway: Key immunization schedule: flu annually (all ≥6 mo); Tdap in pregnancy (27–36w); pneumococcal at 65+; Shingrix (zoster) at 50+, 2-dose series; Tdap once as adult, Td booster q10y.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with a newly placed right subclavian central venous catheter. To reduce the risk of central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI), which bundle element is MOST important during dressing changes?

options.text: Change the dressing every 24 hours regardless of appearance
Use chlorhexidine-based antiseptic and maintain strict sterile technique
Apply antibiotic ointment to the insertion site with each dressing change
Flush the line with normal saline immediately before accessing the port

questions.rationale: The CLABSI prevention bundle includes: hand hygiene, maximal sterile barrier precautions, chlorhexidine skin antisepsis, optimal catheter site selection, and daily review of catheter necessity. Chlorhexidine-based antiseptic (not povidone-iodine) is the evidence-based standard for site care because it has sustained antimicrobial activity. Dressings should be changed every 7 days (transparent) or every 2 days (gauze), or when wet/loose/soiled — not every 24 hours (A). Antibiotic ointment (C) is not recommended and can promote resistant organisms.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "CLABSI bundle: chlorhexidine antiseptic, maximal sterile barriers, hand hygiene, daily necessity assessment, optimal site selection" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: Because the CLABSI prevention bundle includes: hand hygiene, maximal sterile barrier precautions, chlorhexidine skin antisepsis, optimal catheter site selection suggests infection progression, "Apply antibiotic ointment to the insertion site with each dressing change" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: Because the CLABSI prevention bundle includes: hand hygiene, maximal sterile barrier precautions, chlorhexidine skin antisepsis, optimal catheter site selection suggests infection progression, "Flush the line with normal saline immediately before accessing the port" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: CLABSI bundle: chlorhexidine antiseptic, maximal sterile barriers, hand hygiene, daily necessity assessment, optimal site selection.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An 83-year-old patient with hypertension, chronic insomnia, chronic pain, depression, and GERD takes: amlodipine, diphenhydramine (for sleep), amitriptyline, ibuprofen, and omeprazole. Using Beers Criteria principles, which medications present the GREATEST risk for this elderly patient?

options.text: Omeprazole and amlodipine — both increase fall risk and should be avoided in elderly
Diphenhydramine and amitriptyline — both are highly anticholinergic and explicitly listed as inappropriate in the AGS Beers Criteria
Ibuprofen alone — GI bleeding is the only significant concern in this patient
Amlodipine and ibuprofen — calcium channel blockers are contraindicated over age 80

questions.rationale: The 2023 AGS Beers Criteria identifies medications potentially inappropriate in adults ≥65. Diphenhydramine (first-generation antihistamine) and amitriptyline (tricyclic antidepressant) are both strongly anticholinergic and explicitly listed as 'avoid' in elderly. Anticholinergic effects (cumulative burden): cognitive impairment, delirium, urinary retention, constipation, dry mouth, tachycardia, blurred vision, and increased fall risk. Amitriptyline additionally causes orthostatic hypotension (fall risk), cardiac conduction abnormalities, and QT prolongation. Additional concerns in this patient: ibuprofen (NSAID) — risk of GI bleeding (especially with GERD), renal impairment, and cardiovascular events (also Beers listed — prefer acetaminophen). Safer alternatives: melatonin or low-dose doxepin for insomnia; SSRIs/SNRIs for depression (avoid TCAs); acetaminophen for pain.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Diphenhydramine and amitriptyline — both are highly anticholinergic and explicitly listed as inappropriate in the AGS" fits the safer response to additional concerns in this patient: ibuprofen (NSAID) — risk of GI bleeding (especially with GERD), renal impairment.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Diphenhydramine and amitriptyline — both are highly anticholinergic and explicitly listed as inappropriate in the AGS" fits the safer response to an 83-year-old patient with hypertension, chronic insomnia, chronic pain, depression.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "An 83-year-old patient with hypertension, chronic insomnia, chronic pain, depression" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Beers Criteria: anticholinergics (diphenhydramine, TCAs like amitriptyline) are highest risk in elderly — cause delirium, falls, urinary retention. NSAIDs also Beers-listed. Review medications at every hospitalization.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An RN who regularly works on a medical-surgical unit is floated to the cardiac ICU. Which action by the floating RN is most appropriate regarding scope of limitations?

options.text: Refuse the assignment because floating to a specialty unit is unsafe
Accept the assignment and manage all CICU patients including titrating vasopressors and managing IABP
Inform the charge nurse of any tasks outside her competence, accept patients within her skills, and request supervision for unfamiliar procedures
Accept the assignment only if she is designated as a second nurse, not as primary nurse

questions.rationale: Floating RNs are obligated to inform the charge nurse of limitations in specialty skills. They cannot refuse the assignment categorically (A) — refusing is appropriate only if the assignment is unsafe based on skills AND the unit cannot accommodate limitations. They must not perform tasks outside their competence independently (B). Accepting with disclosure of limitations and requesting supervision (C) is the professional and legally safe approach. The float nurse should receive a patient assignment matched to her competency level, with support for unfamiliar procedures. Simply being a 'second nurse' (D) does not adequately protect the patient or satisfy professional accountability.

distractorRationales.a: "Refuse the assignment because floating to a specialty unit is unsafe" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "An RN who regularly works on a medical-surgical unit is floated to the cardiac ICU" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.b: Because an RN who regularly works on a medical-surgical unit is floated to the cardiac ICU suggests circulation or perfusion, "Accept the assignment and manage all CICU patients including titrating vasopressors and managing IABP" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: "Accept the assignment only if she is designated as a second nurse, not as primary nurse" misses the bedside risk because an RN who regularly works on a medical-surgical unit is floated to the cardiac ICU points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Float nurses: disclose competency limitations to charge nurse, accept assignments within skills, request supervision — outright refusal is not appropriate unless assignment is unsafe and no accommodation is possible.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: After fundal massage and oxytocin, a postpartum patient's uterus remains atonic and bleeding continues. The provider orders methylergonovine (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM. Which patient condition is an absolute contraindication to this medication?

options.text: History of asthma
Hypertension with BP currently 158/100 mmHg
Breastfeeding
Gestational diabetes

questions.rationale: Methylergonovine (Methergine) is an ergot alkaloid uterotonic that causes powerful vasoconstriction. It is absolutely contraindicated in hypertension (including pre-eclampsia) because it can cause severe hypertensive crisis, stroke, and coronary artery spasm. Asthma (A) is a relative contraindication for prostaglandins (like carboprost/Hemabate) but not for Methergine. Breastfeeding (C) is not a contraindication — it is safe during breastfeeding. Gestational diabetes (D) is not a contraindication.

distractorRationales.a: Because it is absolutely contraindicated in hypertension (including pre-eclampsia) because it can cause severe hypertensive crisis, stroke suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, "History of asthma" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.c: "Breastfeeding" misses the bedside risk because it is absolutely contraindicated in hypertension (including pre-eclampsia) because it can cause severe hypertensive crisis, stroke points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.d: "Gestational diabetes" misses the bedside risk because methylergonovine (Methergine) is absolutely contraindicated in hypertension — causes severe vasospasm and hypertensive crisis points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Methylergonovine (Methergine) is absolutely contraindicated in hypertension — causes severe vasospasm and hypertensive crisis.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient 4 days after bowel surgery. The patient has not passed flatus, bowel sounds are hypoactive in all quadrants, and the abdomen is mildly distended. Vital signs are stable. The nurse CORRECTLY identifies this presentation as:

options.text: Paralytic ileus — expected post-operative finding
Small bowel obstruction requiring immediate NG tube decompression
Peritonitis requiring emergent surgical consultation
Large bowel obstruction requiring CT scan and surgical consult

questions.rationale: Paralytic ileus (adynamic ileus) is an expected complication in the first 3–5 days after abdominal or bowel surgery, caused by handling of the bowel and anesthetic effects. It presents with absent or hypoactive bowel sounds, lack of flatus, and mild distension without severe pain, fever, or hemodynamic instability. Mechanical obstruction (B, D) presents with high-pitched borborygmi, crampy pain, and vomiting — not simply hypoactive sounds. Peritonitis (C) would have fever, rigid abdomen, and rebound tenderness.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Small bowel obstruction requiring immediate NG tube decompression" moves care toward routine care before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Peritonitis requiring emergent surgical consultation" moves care toward waiting for a callback before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it relies on notification without the bedside safety step. "Paralytic ileus — expected post-operative finding" fits the safer response to mild distension without severe pain, fever, or hemodynamic instability.

questions.takeaway: Ileus vs obstruction: ileus = absent/hypoactive sounds, mild distension, expected post-op; obstruction = high-pitched sounds, crampy pain, vomiting.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient arrives in the ED with sudden onset of right-sided facial droop, right arm weakness, and slurred speech that began 90 minutes ago. NIHSS score is 12. CT head is negative for hemorrhage. The patient's blood pressure is 178/96 mmHg. Which intervention is the highest priority?

options.text: Begin antihypertensive therapy immediately to bring BP below 140/90 mmHg
Prepare the patient for IV thrombolysis with alteplase (tPA) — notify the provider of eligibility and start the time-critical pre-tPA workup
Administer aspirin 325 mg orally for antiplatelet therapy
Obtain emergent MRI brain to confirm ischemic versus hemorrhagic stroke before any treatment

questions.rationale: This patient presents within the 4.5-hour tPA window with an ischemic stroke (CT negative for hemorrhage) and a significant neurological deficit (NIHSS 12). IV alteplase is the priority intervention — every minute of delay worsens outcomes ('time is brain' — ~1.9 million neurons lost per minute). In ischemic stroke with tPA candidacy, blood pressure is treated only if > 185/110 mmHg; at 178/96, aggressive BP lowering is not indicated (it can worsen ischemia). Aspirin is not given within 24 hours of tPA. CT is already done — MRI is not required for tPA administration when CT excludes hemorrhage. The nurse should mobilize the stroke team and prepare for tPA.

distractorRationales.a: "Begin antihypertensive therapy immediately to bring BP below 140/90 mmHg" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "5-hour tPA window with an ischemic stroke (CT negative for hemorrhage) and a significant neurological deficit (NIHSS 12)" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: Because ischemic stroke + within 4 suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, "Administer aspirin 325 mg orally for antiplatelet therapy" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Obtain emergent MRI brain to confirm ischemic versus hemorrhagic stroke before any treatment" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before bleeding-risk intervention.

questions.takeaway: Ischemic stroke + within 4.5h window + no hemorrhage on CT = tPA eligibility evaluation is the priority. Time is brain. Do NOT lower BP below 185/110 before tPA. Aspirin is contraindicated within 24h of tPA.

visualRationale.title: Neurological / Stroke
visualRationale.caption: Ischemic stroke + within 4.5h window + no hemorrhage on CT = tPA eligibility evaluation is the priority. Time is brain. Do NOT lower BP below 185/110 before tPA. Aspirin is contraindicated within 24h of tPA.
visualRationale.conclusion: Ischemic stroke + within 4.5h window + no hemorrhage on CT = tPA eligibility evaluation is the priority. Time is brain. Do NOT lower BP below 185/110 before tPA. Aspirin is contraindicated within 24h of tPA.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old with no prior medical or anesthesia history is 30 minutes into general anesthesia for appendectomy. The PACU nurse is called to the OR: temperature 39.9°C and rising, masseter muscle rigidity (jaw locked), HR 162 and irregular, SpO2 87%, and ETCO2 82 mmHg. What is the priority action?

options.text: Administer acetaminophen IV and cooling blankets for a likely febrile surgical response
Activate the malignant hyperthermia (MH) protocol: stop all triggering agents, hyperventilate with 100% O2, administer dantrolene sodium 2.5 mg/kg IV, and call for the MH cart
Administer succinylcholine to relieve the masseter rigidity and improve ventilation
Treat the hyperkalemia first with calcium gluconate — MH-induced hyperkalemia is the immediate threat

questions.rationale: Malignant hyperthermia (MH) is a life-threatening pharmacogenetic disorder triggered in susceptible individuals (RYR1 gene mutation) by volatile anesthetic agents (sevoflurane, desflurane, halothane) and succinylcholine. Uncontrolled calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum causes: sustained muscle contraction → hyperthermia (temperature rises 1°C every 5 minutes if untreated), diffuse rigidity, masseter spasm (early sign), respiratory and metabolic acidosis (CO2 soaring), rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, and ventricular dysrhythmias. Untreated MH mortality exceeds 80%. Protocol: (1) Stop ALL triggering agents immediately; (2) Hyperventilate with 100% O2 at 2-3× normal minute ventilation (flush volatile agents, treat hypercapnia); (3) Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus — repeat every 5 minutes until crisis resolves (max ~10 mg/kg); (4) Cooling measures; (5) Treat hyperkalemia, acidosis, and dysrhythmias as they arise. Succinylcholine IS a triggering agent — administering it would worsen MH.

distractorRationales.a: "Administer acetaminophen IV and cooling blankets for a likely febrile surgical response" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "(2) Hyperventilate with 100% O2 at 2-3× normal minute ventilation (flush volatile agents, treat hypercapnia)" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Administer succinylcholine to relieve the masseter rigidity and improve ventilation" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "9°C and rising, masseter muscle rigidity (jaw locked), HR 162 and irregular, SpO2 87%" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Activate the malignant hyperthermia (MH) protocol: stop all triggering agents, hyperventilate with 100% O2, administer dantrolene" fits the safer response to protocol: (1) Stop ALL triggering agents immediately.

questions.takeaway: MH: rising temp + masseter rigidity + high ETCO2 + tachycardia during general anesthesia. STOP triggering agents immediately. Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV STAT (repeat q5 min). Succinylcholine triggers MH — never give.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes patient about glipizide (a sulfonylurea). Which statement is MOST important to include?

options.text: This medication works best when taken with a large meal
This medication can cause low blood sugar — carry a fast-acting sugar source at all times
This medication does not cause weight gain
Blood glucose monitoring is not necessary since this medication automatically maintains glucose levels

questions.rationale: Sulfonylureas (glipizide, glyburide, glimepiride) work by stimulating pancreatic beta cells to release insulin, independent of blood glucose levels. This means they can cause hypoglycemia even when the patient is not eating — unlike some other diabetes medications. Patients must be educated to always carry fast-acting glucose (glucose tablets, juice) and to recognize hypoglycemia symptoms (shakiness, diaphoresis, confusion). Glipizide is taken 30 minutes before meals (not with a large meal) to time insulin release with carbohydrate absorption. Sulfonylureas often cause modest weight gain. Blood glucose monitoring remains important for safety and titration.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "This medication can cause low blood sugar — carry a fast-acting sugar source at all times" fits the safer response to patients must be educated to always carry fast-acting glucose (glucose tablets, juice) and to recognize hypoglycemia symptoms (shakiness.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "This medication can cause low blood sugar — carry a fast-acting sugar source at all times" fits the safer response to sulfonylureas = insulin secretagogues = hypoglycemia risk even when not eating.
distractorRationales.d: "Blood glucose monitoring is not necessary since this medication automatically maintains glucose levels" relies on notification without the bedside safety step. The cue "Sulfonylureas (glipizide, glyburide, glimepiride) work by stimulating pancreatic beta cells to release insulin, independent of blood glucose levels" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before waiting for a callback.

questions.takeaway: Sulfonylureas = insulin secretagogues = hypoglycemia risk even when not eating. Always carry fast-acting carbs. Glipizide: 30 min before meals. Monitor BG. Watch for weight gain. Avoid skipping meals while on this medication.
